pattern: Walking both strings with two pointers and only advancing the target pointer when the current source character matches finds exactly how much of the target is already satisfied as a subsequence, and whatever is left over is the minimum that must be appended.
hints: You're not looking for where t appears inside s -- you're checking how much of t can be matched, in order, by scanning through s once.
Walk s with one pointer; keep a second pointer into t that only moves forward when the current character of s equals the current character of t.
Once you reach the end of s, whatever characters of t the second pointer hasn't reached yet are exactly the ones you must append -- the answer is simply how many characters of t are left unmatched.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, partial match needs the rest appended
statement example, t is already a full subsequence
statement example, no character of t matches at all
single matching character in both strings, nothing to append
single non-matching character in both strings, must append the whole target
t's letters exist in s but out of order, so none of them count as a matching prefix
s and t are identical, entire t already a subsequence
repeated characters in s must still advance t's pointer only once per matched character

stress.description: random lowercase strings of varying lengths, some sharing a common alphabet-restricted vocabulary to force partial matches
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    m = rng.randint(1, 100000)
    alphabet_size = rng.choice([2, 5, 26])
    letters = [chr(ord('a') + i) for i in range(alphabet_size)]
    s = ''.join(rng.choice(letters) for _ in range(n))
    t = ''.join(rng.choice(letters) for _ in range(m))
    return [s, t]
